ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for spaghetti bolognese; FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta dishes — mixed pasta with meat sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers pasta dishes with meat sauce as the closest available category; no exact bolognese-specific code exists in the public FoodEx2 browser.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of spaghetti bolognese as served in a Singapore casual dining or food court setting weighs approximately 350 g, comprising boiled spaghetti topped with a slow-cooked ground beef and tomato sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Spaghetti with meat sauce, SR Legacy composite (FDC ID 168927)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input values were sourced from GrabFood listing (ai_generated, confidence 0.7), a forbidden primary nutrient source. Per_100g values have been replaced with data cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite for spaghetti with meat sauce (FDC ID 168927 and related composites). Input energy of 242.86 kcal/100g was implausibly high and corrected to 142 kcal/100g per USDA FDC range. Input fat of 17.14 g/100g was corrected to 5.0 g/100g. Input calcium of 85.71 mg/100g was corrected to 35 mg/100g. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not carry a direct bolognese entry; USDA SR Legacy composite used as primary approved-source anchor.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for spaghetti bolognese as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 based on the GI of cooked white spaghetti al dente (GI ~45–49, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate contributor. The meat sauce component (ground beef, tomato) contributes negligible additional carbohydrate and does not materially alter the overall GI. GL per 350 g serving calculated as: (45 × 54.25 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 24.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 343 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced added salt and omit or reduce Parmesan cheese garnish to lower the sodium load of this dish; pairing with a potassium-rich side such as a green salad may partially offset the sodium effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat spaghetti has a GI of approximately 45 (low), but a 350 g serving delivers approximately 54 g of carbohydrate, producing a glycemic load of approximately 24, which is high and requires portion control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g, substitute wholemeal or legume-based pasta to reduce GI and increase fibre, and ensure the meat sauce is prepared without added sugar to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 1.8 g per 100 g (6.3 g per 350 g serving) from ground beef and Parmesan cheese contributes to LDL-raising saturated fat intake; sodium at 343 mg per 100 g also elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean ground beef (≤10% fat) or substitute with ground turkey or lentils, reduce Parmesan cheese, and use olive oil as the cooking fat to improve the saturated-to-unsaturated fat ratio.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ground beef is a red meat high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); saturated fat content further promotes systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute ground beef with ground turkey, chicken, or lentils, retain olive oil and herbs (oregano, basil) for their anti-inflammatory properties, and monitor symptoms if tomato-based sauce triggers nightshade sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spaghetti Bolognese (also spelled Bolognaise) is a classic Italian-American pasta dish widely served in Singapore's casual dining restaurants, food courts, and Western-cuisine stalls. The dish consists of boiled spaghetti — typically white wheat pasta cooked al dente — topped with a slow-cooked, rich meat sauce (ragù alla bolognese) made from ground beef, crushed tomatoes, onion, garlic, carrot, celery, olive oil, and dried herbs such as oregano and basil. In Singapore adaptations, the dish is often garnished with grated Parmesan or mozzarella cheese. The sauce is simmered for 30–60 minutes to develop depth of flavour, resulting in a savoury, slightly sweet, umami-rich profile with a tender, meaty texture. Nutritionally, a 350 g serving provides approximately 497 kcal, 30 g protein, 17.5 g fat, and 54 g carbohydrate. Sodium is moderately high at approximately 1,200 mg per serving, making portion control important for hypertensive individuals. The GI of the dish is approximately 45 (low), driven by the al dente spaghetti base, but the glycemic load per serving is high (~24) due to the large carbohydrate mass. Saturated fat from ground beef and cheese warrants attention for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and contains gluten and dairy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a plate of bolognese?
What is the GI of spaghetti bolognese?
Can I eat bolognese if I have high blood pressure?
Is bolognese good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood, a forbidden primary nutrient source. Per_100g values have been replaced with data cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite for spaghetti with meat sauce (FDC ID 168927). Confidence is medium because no exact Singapore-specific bolognese entry exists in HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD; USDA SR Legacy composite used as the closest approved-source anchor.
notes: 1. Input energy value of 242.86 kcal/100g was flagged as implausibly high for a standard spaghetti bolognese (typical range 130–155 kcal/100g per USDA FDC); corrected to 142 kcal/100g. Input fat value of 17.14 g/100g was also high; corrected to 5.0 g/100g consistent with USDA SR Legacy composite for spaghetti with meat sauce. Input calcium value of 85.71 mg/100g was higher than USDA data; corrected to 35 mg/100g. All corrections documented here. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry used as primary anchor. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy composite entry for spaghetti with meat sauce as accessed via USDA FoodData Central. 3. GI estimated at 45 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for cooked white spaghetti al dente; no direct composite GI for bolognese available. GL per 350 g serving = 24.4. 4. Input health_suitability references 'potato fries' and 'nacho cheese sauce' which are not standard bolognese components; these references have been removed as they appear to be copy-paste errors from a different dish record. Health context has been rewritten to reflect standard spaghetti bolognese. 5. Input had two portions both marked is_default: true; serving size set to 350 g (1 plate) as the primary default. 6. Halal status marked false as standard bolognese uses ground beef which may not be halal-certified in all Singapore outlets; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 7. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 per validator feedback; these represent parent/broader categories rather than exact matches.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Spaghetti Bolognese' used. Input name 'Bolognaise' retained as primary alias. Mandatory local-language names present for Singaporean Western dish: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g all present and numeric. Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite for spaghetti with meat sauce (FDC ID 168927). Input values corrected where inconsistent with approved source. nutrient_source cites USDA FoodData Central as the approved primary source; GrabFood (forbidden source) explicitly replaced.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.4), sodium_mg (343.0), saturated_fat_g (1.8), sugar_g (2.3) all present as numeric values consistent with USDA FDC data.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index: 45 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white spaghetti al dente). gi_category: low. glycemic_load_per_serving: 24.4 (per 350 g serving). gi_source fully documented with analogous estimate basis explained. GI source cites University of Sydney GI Database via peer-reviewed journal (Atkinson et al., AJCN 2021).

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) used as closest_parent with gap note. FoodEx2 A0CKP (pasta dishes with meat sauce) used as closest_parent with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. Match types corrected to 'closest_parent' (valid enum value) per validator feedback. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above the 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context (Singapore), clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly with machine key 'type2_diabetes'. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis included from input.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 13 entries covering alternate English names (Bolognaise, Spag bol, Meat sauce pasta, Pasta Bolognese, Bolognese spaghetti), Chinese script forms, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized form. All aliases_by_language string values verified present verbatim in flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected nutrient_source to cite USDA FoodData Central (approved source) explicitly, removing GrabFood as primary evidence. Corrected per_100g values to align with USDA SR Legacy composite. Corrected ontology match_type values from 'analogous_estimate' to 'broader'.

Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the valid enum values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by g5_ontology_honesty. Corrected glycemic_load_per_serving from 9.9 (per-100g basis) to 24.4 (per 350 g serving basis) for accuracy. Confirmed nutrient_source title and URL reference only USDA FoodData Central as the approved source.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8. Previous iteration (iter02) failed on g2_approved_source_compliance (forbidden source in nutrient_source) and g5_ontology_honesty (invalid match_type enum values 'broader' and 'analogous_estimate'). This iteration corrects ontology match_type values to 'closest_parent' (valid enum), confirms nutrient_source cites only USDA FoodData Central as the approved primary source, and corrects GL per serving to 24.4. All hard gates now pass and all 8 quality criteria are satisfied.
